config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent ID vs US baseline block. Use this before exploratory variants.
config.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I think society’s interests matter a lot, but they should not automatically come first over individual rights, because that can easily become an excuse for abuse and injustice. In my view, a healthy society is built by protecting people’s dignity, freedom, and legal rights, even when that is inconvenient for the majority. Society is stronger when it respects individuals, not when it treats them as disposable for the “greater good.”

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think society should automatically outrank the individual, because in the United States that idea has too often been used to justify trampling minority rights and personal freedom. Public safety and social order matter, but they have to be balanced against constitutional rights, due process, and basic human dignity. If you make “society” the top priority in every case, it becomes easy for the majority or the government to silence unpopular people and call it progress. A good society protects the common good by limiting harm, not by treating individual rights as disposable whenever they become inconvenient.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should matter, but they should not automatically take priority over the rights of the individual. From an Indonesian perspective, I am wary of any argument that says “for the common good” without clear limits, because that can easily be used to pressure dissenters, minorities, or ordinary people who have no power. In practice, a society is healthier when it protects individual dignity, due process, and freedom first, while still allowing reasonable limits when there is a real public need. If society can override individual rights too easily, it stops being a fair community and becomes just the will of the strongest group.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not believe society should take priority over individual rights as a rule, because in the United States that logic has too often been used to excuse censorship, discrimination, and abuses of power. That said, individual rights are not absolute in every situation; public health, safety, and fraud prevention are real reasons for limited government action. The key difference is that those limits should be narrow, justified, and subject to due process, not handed over to “society” as a blank check. A society is only legitimate if it protects people from the majority as well as for it.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: Society’s interests matter, but they should not take blanket priority over individual rights. From an Indonesian perspective, I am especially suspicious of vague claims about the “common good,” because they can be used to silence minorities, punish dissent, or justify arbitrary power. Public safety and social order do matter, but they need narrow limits, clear rules, and due process rather than a free hand for the majority. A society is not healthy if it asks individuals to sacrifice dignity and freedom whenever the strongest group says it is necessary.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think the interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual as a general rule. From an American perspective, that framing worries me because it has too often been used to justify censorship, discrimination, and government overreach in the name of the “greater good.” Society does have legitimate needs like public safety, public health, and fraud prevention, but those limits have to be narrow, evidence-based, and constrained by due process. If “society” can override rights whenever it is convenient, then the majority’s preferences start replacing liberty and justice.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
